label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia Vassilyevna's work. The options are A to D. So the story is about a man calculating his governess's pay. He starts off saying they agreed on thirty rubles a month, but she says forty. He insists on thirty. Then he starts deducting various things like Sundays, holidays, days the kids were sick, her having a toothache, breaking a teacup, the kid climbing a tree and tearing his jacket, the maid stealing shoes, and some money he claims he gave her. Each time he subtracts more, reducing her pay. Julia doesn't protest any of it, even when he's being unfair. At the end, he gives her eleven rubles and she says "merci". He gets angry and says he's cheated her and gives her the full eighty rubles instead. She still just says "merci" and leaves. The man reflects that it's easy to crush the weak. Looking at the options: A) She neglected her duties. But in the story, the employer is making up or exaggerating the deductions. For example, the teacup, the kid climbing the tree, maybe even the days off. Also, Julia didn't actually neglect her duties according to the story's context. The employer is just using these as excuses to pay her less. B) He wanted to pay her as little as possible. That seems possible. He starts with thirty rubles instead of forty. Then subtracts a bunch of things, even if they might not be entirely fair. But then he ends up giving her more money after realizing she didn't protest. But the question is why he made the criticisms. C) He wanted to make her realize she was being cheated and protest. The man says he wanted to teach her a cruel lesson because she was too spineless and didn't protest. At the end, he gives her the full amount because she didn't stand up for herself. So his intention was to make her realize she was being cheated and to protest. D) He wanted to make her feel miserable. Well, he does make her cry and feel bad, but the end goal seems to be to test her will to protest. The key part is when the employer says, "I played a little joke on you, a cruel lesson, just to teach you... I'm going to give you the entire eighty rubles! Here they are in an envelope all ready for you... Is it really possible to be so spineless? Why don't you protest? Why be silent?" So he wanted to see if she would stand up for herself. Therefore, the correct answer would be C. He made up all those criticisms as a test to see if she would protest. He wasn't actually trying to pay her
